rationale_for_baseline_tier: Active but controllable haemorrhage (a slow-to-steady venous ooze, patient haemodynamically stable) from a suspected high-risk skin malignancy on the dorsal hand, in a frail elderly nursing-home resident on a direct oral anticoagulant (apixaban), where basic nursing pressure had not yet achieved hemostasis. Definitive hemostasis (chemical cautery / professional dressing / pressure bandage) and a fast-track excision/biopsy + nodal-assessment referral pathway require URGENT same-day in-person care — telehealth cannot cauterise, dress, bandage or biopsy. It is not an immediate threat to life (no shock, no airway/circulatory compromise, a controllable ooze) so it is NOT baseline T5; but it is too acute, too suspicious, and too anticoagulated for routine-only management, so T3 (urgent same-day / urgent care) is the correct baseline, with a LOW threshold to escalate to ED (T4) if firm sustained pressure and elevation fail or blood-loss signs appear.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Bleeding not controlled after a full 20 minutes of firm continuous direct pressure with the hand elevated, or the dressing/bandage saturating and dripping continuously
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: If firm pressure and elevation for 20 minutes fail to control it, arrange transfer to the emergency department for surgical hemostasis
escalation_edges.rationale: Uncontrolled bleeding despite adequate first aid, prolonged by a direct oral anticoagulant, requires definitive surgical/procedural hemostasis that only an ED or in-person proceduralist can provide.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the bleeding hasn't stopped after 20 minutes of firm, unbroken pressure with the hand raised — or the bandage soaks through and keeps dripping — the nurses should arrange transfer to the emergency department for the bleeding to be sealed.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Signs of significant blood loss — dizziness or lightheadedness, pallor, a fast or pounding heartbeat, feeling faint or clammy
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek emergency assessment the same hour if these appear
escalation_edges.rationale: These indicate the bleed has caused clinically meaningful blood loss in a frail anticoagulated patient and needs urgent assessment and hemostasis.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he becomes dizzy, pale, faint or his heart starts racing, treat it as significant blood loss — get him to the emergency department straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Collapse, loss of consciousness, or torrential/spurting bleeding that cannot be controlled at all
escalation_edges.rationale: Collapse or truly uncontrollable haemorrhage is a life-threatening emergency requiring an ambulance; this is the ceiling escalation, not the baseline response to a controllable venous ooze.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he collapses, becomes unresponsive, or the bleeding is heavy and simply will not stop with firm pressure, call 000 for an ambulance right away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The lesion itself — a rapidly-growing, indurated, ulcerating hand lump with a palpable gland under the arm — regardless of the bleeding
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Urgent in-person skin/surgical review with a fast-track excision/biopsy and nodal-assessment referral within days, even after bleeding is controlled
escalation_edges.rationale: The suspicious high-risk lesion needs urgent tissue diagnosis, excision and staging of the palpable node independent of the acute bleed; controlling the bleeding does not close the case.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Even once the bleeding settles, this lump needs an urgent in-person skin or surgical doctor to look at it, take a biopsy, plan removal and check the gland under the arm — please make sure that urgent referral is booked within days.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Frail older adult, residential aged-care resident using a walking frame, on a direct oral anticoagulant (apixaban)
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Keep the threshold to confirm the bleeding is truly controlled low, and ensure on-site nursing support is arranged for firm pressure, elevation and dressing care; but also avoid an unnecessary 000 ambulance transfer, which is itself distressing and harmful for a frail resident when the bleed is controllable in place. Balance urgent lesion review and a low ED-escalation threshold (given anticoagulation) against over-medicalising a stable patient.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: A facility registered nurse is present and can perform firm pressure, elevation, dressing changes and monitoring
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Leverage the on-site RN to deliver firm continuous direct pressure with elevation and ongoing wound care and to escalate if triggers are met — this supports safe management in place while urgent in-person clinician review and a fast-track referral are arranged.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Have the on-site nurse apply firm, continuous, uninterrupted pressure over gauze to the spot for a full 20 minutes without peeking, with the hand elevated above heart level, then keep the pressure dressing dry and intact for about three days. Arrange an urgent same-day in-person review (facility GP, after-hours medical service, or urgent care) to seal the bleeding point properly and to organise a fast-track referral for biopsy, removal of the lump and checking of the gland under the arm. This is urgent but not, while he is stable and the bleeding is a controllable ooze, a 000 ambulance emergency.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Urgent same-day facility GP / after-hours medical service / urgent care review; emergency department if pressure fails or blood-loss signs appear; call 000 only for collapse or uncontrollable bleeding
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If after hours and the bleeding is controlled with firm pressure, elevation and a dressing, arrange the earliest same-day in-person review; if the bleeding cannot be controlled by 20 minutes of firm pressure and elevation, or he shows signs of significant blood loss, go to the emergency department.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Document the lesion (a long-standing actinic keratosis on the dorsal left hand that rapidly grew over six weeks into a firm indurated keratotic nodule, now ulcerated with a necrotic base and contact bleeding, with a palpable 1.5 cm left axillary node), daily apixaban 5 mg twice daily for atrial fibrillation, and request an URGENT fast-track skin-cancer / surgical review for biopsy, excision and regional nodal assessment given the high-risk features.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Reassuring and discharging with only self-care or routine advice for a suspicious, rapidly-growing, actively-bleeding hand malignancy in a frail anticoagulated patient.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An invasive skin cancer is missed and left to grow, invade and potentially metastasise, and an anticoagulated frail patient is left with an unresolved bleeding lesion and no referral.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Treating it as a routine 24-48 hour GP problem without arranging same-day in-person hemostasis when basic nursing pressure had already failed to stop the bleed in an anticoagulated patient.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling a 000 ambulance as the baseline response — a distressing emergency transfer of a frail resident and misuse of ambulance resources for a controllable venous ooze in a haemodynamically stable patient. (Directing to ED (T4) for definitive hemostasis is a defensible escalation given the DOAC and is not scored as a defect.)
